titleStmt.title: Letter to the Under Secretary from Edward O'Farrell, 20 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Edward O'Farrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
The Under Secretary
Chief Secretary's Office
Dublin Castle 20th June, 1916
the following number quoted
9729 CIRCULAR TO CROWN AND SESSIONAL CROWN SOLICITORS. Sir, With a view to avoiding unecessary expenditure in
the service of Crown Summonses, Crown and Sessional Crown
Solicitors should comply, whenever possible, with the
following instructons, which have been approved by the
Attorney-General: - I. A summons for a witness in the civil or Military
service of the Crown should be sent by registered
letter address to the Head of the Department, or
the Commanding Office of the Unit, to which the
witness belongs. An acknowledgement of the letter
should be requested. II. Where a constable has made a Deposition in a case,
he should be employed to serve the summonses on
witnesses residing in his or an adjoining Sub-
District. All other summonses for witnesses resident
in Ireland should be served by the Constabulary of
the Sub-District wherein the witnesses reside. No
Constable who has not made a Deposition in the case
should attend the Court to prove service unless he is
required to do so by the Crown Solicitor. III.A summons for a witness resident in Great Britain
should be sent for service to the Chief Constable of
the District in which the resident resides, with a
request for an acknowledgement. An intimation
should be sent to the Constable serving the summons
should not attend in Ireland to prove service unless
required to do so by the Crown Solicitor. <
In reference to Circular of 19th May, 1915, notify-
ing that the Writs of Habeas Corpus are no longer necessary, the#
Attorney-General directs that the Circular in question is not
to be treated as applicable in Change of Venue Cases, and
that, in future, in these cases, the former practice is to
be followed and Writs of Habeas Corpus applied for to bring
Defendants to Dublin in order that they may plead. I am
Sir,
Your obedient Servant,
E.O'FARRELL.
CIRCULAR TO CROWN AND SESSIONAL CROWN SOLICITORS Dublin Castle, June, 1916 Sir, With a view to avoiding unnecessary expenditure in
the service of Crown Summonses, Crown and Sessional Crown
Solicitors should comply, whenever possible, with the
following instructions, which have been approved by the
Attorney-General— I. A summons for a witness in the civil or Military
service of the Crown should be sent by registered
letter address to the Head of the Department, or
the Commanding Office of the Unit, to which the
witness belongs. An acknowledgement of the letter
should be requested. II. Where a constable has made a Deposition in a case,
he should be employed to serve the summonses on
witnesses residing in his or an adjoining Sub-
District. All other summonses for witnesses
resident in Ireland should be served by the Con-
stabulary of the Sub-District wherein the witnesses
reside. No Constable who has not made a Deposition
in the case should attend the Court to prove service
unless he is required to do so by the Crown Solicitor. III.A summons for a witness resident in Great Britian
should be sent for service to the Chief Constable of
the District in which the resident resides, with a
request for an acknowledgement: but in Liverpool,
Glasgow, and the summons should be
sent through the County Inspector, R.I.C. for service
by a member of the Irish Police resident in the
places named. . An intimation should be sent that the
Constable serving the summons should not attend in
Ireland to prove service unless required to do so by
the Crown Solicitor. In reference to Circular of 19th May, 1915, notify-
ing that Writs of Habeas Corpus are no longer necessary,
the Attorney-General directs that the Circular in question
is not to be treated as applicable in Change of Venue cases,
and that, in future, in these cases, the former practice is
to be followed and Writs of Habeas Corpus applied for to
bring Defendants to Dublin in order that they may plead. I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant, E. O'Farrell
